summary: Jeff Atwood argues that while routers have become commoditized household necessities, the best ones run open-source firmware like DD-WRT or Tomato. Frustrated by his aging router's stale proprietary firmware, he researched alternatives and discovered that open-source firmware transforms ordinary hardware into feature-rich, perpetually-updated networking powerhouses. He recommends the Buffalo Nfiniti router running DD-WRT as the ideal combination of affordable commodity hardware and powerful open-source software. The post concludes that this pairing of commodity hardware with open-source firmware represents the future not just of routers, but of all commodity hardware.
key_points: Routers have evolved from exotic network equipment to essential household items that everyone with an internet connection needs
Proprietary router firmware becomes abandoned as vendors move on to newer models, leaving users with outdated and potentially insecure devices
Open-source firmware projects like DD-WRT and Tomato provide perpetual community updates and dramatically expanded features
The combination of affordable commodity hardware with open-source firmware delivers better results than expensive proprietary solutions
Building a dedicated x86 PC for routing is unnecessarily complex when commodity routers have sufficient CPU power and memory
money_quotes: Anyone who pays for an internet connection needs a router.
The best router models all run open source firmware!
The truly great routers are available in 'awesome' edition.
I was growing increasingly uncomfortable with the status quo. Frankly, the prospect of three year old hardware with year old firmware gives me the heebie-jeebies.
DD-WRT scratches every geek itch I have.
Dedicating a whole x86 PC to routing is power inefficient, overly complex, and awkward.
The magic router formula these days is a combination of commodity hardware and open-source firmware.
Thanks to a wide variety of mature commodity hardware choices, plus infinitely and perpetually updated open source router firmware, I'm happy to report that now everyone can have a great router.
key_insight: Open-source firmware transforms abandoned commodity hardware into perpetually-updated, feature-rich devices—a model that represents the future of all consumer hardware.
